sentences.text: Well, the first step he does, is goes to the Jewish community to talk to them, get some information.
And he comes there to the president, Dr.
Goldberg.
And Dr.
Goldberg is already familiar with my case.
And my father brought with him some pictures and says, Mr.
Goldberg, is it possible that this child, who did so much noise here, is my daughter?
And Mr.
Goldberg looks at the picture.
He said, yes, yes.
The troublemaker is your daughter.
I'm pretty sure.
She looks just like the picture here.
And he said, you have to be very careful when you go there.
I'll assist you.
Because the grandfather, he's a real drunk.
And he's not a very reasonable person.
And Ida is afraid of people, very suspicious child.
You better wear a cross on your neck.
So my father purchased a cross, and came to visit where I lived, [NON-ENGLISH].
And my grandfather comes out when they came.
And the grandfather said, yes, I saved the child, the Jewish child.
I sacrificed my life.
Everything is I, I, I.
And he's responsible for everything.
And he's not giving away this child.
He said, first of all, he said, how do I know you are the father?
So my father shows the pictures.
And he looked at it.
And he said, still, he said, there are cases like that here in Transtahova, that there were other people.
And they get reward for it.
What am I going to get?
My father says, I just came from Russia.
This is the only outfit I have.
I brought with me three kilograms of sugar, because they said there is no sugar in Poland.
He said, I have nothing.
He said, if you have nothing, then we have nothing to talk about.
So Mr.
Goldberg took on the side, my father, and they talked quietly there.
And Mr.
Goldberg talked to Mr.
Maj.
And he promised him that they'll do whatever it's possible.
But it took a long time.
And my father got impatient.
And he went to the court in Transtahova to claim me.
Where in all this was your mother?
My Polish mother was out of the picture for awhile.
She was afraid to come back.
I didn't want to see her.
Right.
I was afraid of her.
In a way I missed her.
But on the other way, I was afraid of her.
She's going to do the same thing all over.
So when my father came to the courts, and he saw the mess there is in court, he had no chance of winning.
So he did come a few times to visit me and promise that he's going to come back with money to Mr.
Maj.
But in between, he wanted to get acquainted with me.
So he came with candy, or with sweets.
And he tried to get with me kind of friendly.
Was he all that time pretending that he was a Catholic?
Yes.
Yes.
He was wearing the cross.
And we had a lot of saints hanging on the wall, I remember.
And he used to take me here, above, and carry me so I can get to the saints and kiss them.
And I went from one to another.
And one day, my father comes to visit with a Dr.
Drucker, a captain.
Who was, I found out later, he organized the orphanage in Zabrze.
He was taking children from Christian families and putting them there.
And then afterwards, he prepared them to take them to Israel.
And he was helping my father to get the funds.
And they went from organization to organization.
My father finally had some sum of money, but not the one that Mr.
Maj demanded.
But when Mr.
Maj saw the money, he was happy to get it.
And by force, I was taken forcefully into a cab, a real cab, screaming and kicking, scratching my father face, and screaming, you Jews!
Too bad Hitler didn't finish you up.
I hate you.
I'm sure that you are not my father.
Whoever you are, leave me alone.
And my father dragged me on the stairs, upstairs in Sosnowiec, to his sister-in-law that he lived temporarily with.
And over there, he had to hold me forcefully.
Because I wanted to run to the window and jump through the window and scream, help.
He told me all the time that he's my father.
But he didn't want to mention the word Jew to me.
Because it was, like, bad word.
At that time, I believe, that in today's money, what he wanted was maybe $100,000.
But he got maybe $5,000.
That's all, yes.
Yeah, but in zloty it's probably a different amount.
And I understand that in two weeks, he drank the money.
And there was nothing left.
My Polish mother did not get a penny.
Because she was in the background.
My father didn't know that she was the one who saved me.
So going back in Sosnowiec.
I was screaming and crying.
And I didn't want to do anything, because I was in panic.
So my aunt and my father decided if they bring some neighbor's children maybe I'll start to play with them and stop.
And they brought some baby, which was very interesting to me.
And I started to play with the baby.
And probably out of being tired and not eating, I fell asleep.
At night my aunt and my father took off my cross from my chain.
And when I woke up and I wanted to pray, I didn't have that cross.
I started to scream again.
I felt like God abandoned me.
Who is going to protect me?
Because I always knew that angels walk behind me.
And Jesus Christ is taking, you know, care of me.
And so on.
So when I did not see that, I was really scared and afraid.
And at that time, I already became very quiet.
I did not scream.
So my father took me out to Jewish organization to look for clothes.
I had no shoes, and very bad clothes, dirty.
You know how children look after the war.
I was so skinny.
I didn't look my age.
At that time I was seven.
I looked like a five-year-old, my father said.
So he took me to those organizations.
And I remember picking up clothes that I wanted.
And I did pick them up.
And in the organizations there were some people talking to my father, what to do with this child.
And they advised him to take me to the orphanage where Captain Drucker has other children.
Which is not far from the city of Sosnowiec.
And he took me on a, on a, a, [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know what you call it.
A train.
And he took me there.
And when he brought me there, there were young ladies who took me in.
And they were very sweet.
And they gave me oranges.
They gave me chocolate.
They came to a big-- like a room where there were clothes sitting in the, in the closets and told me, [NON-ENGLISH], do you want this?
[NON-ENGLISH], do you want that?
And I did, yes.
And I didn't care that my father leaves.
So what?
I didn't want him to be with me.
And when I found out there are other children like myself, I got very friendly with them.
And they showed me how to play piano because I played a little piano.
And they started to teach me how to pray in Hebrew and how to kiss the mezuzah when you come into the house, and all that.
At that time I get by.
My father said that the few weeks when he came to visit me, I was a changed child.
I was happy there.
And the word Jew didn't bother me anymore.
When I found out the other children are Jewish, too, I thought to myself, they're no different than the other one.
And the people here were so nice.
I was never hungry.
I was clean.
My choices were like their wish to do anything for me.
They like me very much.
And they were very nice to each of the children.
I believe at that time, my father registered me there as Ida Paluch.
After the orphanage, my father came and picked me up.
And I think he didn't settle all the business in Breslau, because he was supposed to get married to another lady who was also a survivor of Auschwitz.
He put me in the house of his sister-in-law.
His brother did not survive.
But the sister-in-law survived, Frania.
And I stayed with them.
She already was married to another man.
And they were so nice.
I remember he made me the first pair of red shoes, leather, that I had for a longest time.
And they were made a few sizes bigger, so I can grow into them.
That's my memory from Aunt Frania.
I believe it was a matchmaker, through a matchmaker that-- they kind of match up people that would never fit before, that would never be married before the war, together.
But it was like a horse into a carriage.
It was a fish into a carriage.
If you understand what I mean.
Completely unprepared, just to have a home.
That's why they got married after the war.
Just to resume the normal way of life.
I think that I stayed in the orphanage after Sukkot.
Because I was-- that was my first Jewish holiday that I celebrated.
I remember going with the apple and a stick and a flag and eating in the Sukkot.
I remember that was my first holiday.
And from the pictures that I saw later that I have, I was already wearing winter clothes when I came to Breslau.
And my father was already married to this woman.
And first of all, he wanted to register me to school.
But when they look at me in school, they did not believe that I am at age seven at that time.
So they put me in a kindergarten.
And in kindergarten, it was very nice.
Children were just like myself.
The only thing that everybody spoke Russian.
They were all children survivors of Russia.
And I was the only one who spoke Polish.
But all that evened out in few months when they learned to speak Polish.
No.
I never did.
[LAUGHS] I was always behind because they were already with their background of Russian.
And that was the language that I had to learn from grade fifth up.
And after finishing kindergarten, I went to the first grade in a Jewish school in Breslau.
That was the only Jewish school.
It was called, Sholem Aleichem.
And it was on this street of [NON-ENGLISH], which is a Jewish name.
It was a public school, but only Jewish children attended there.
And we had very good teachers.
They were to us like parents.
And all this-- all the children, they were like one family.
We knew each other.
We knew the parents.
Everybody knew everything about the other person.
And they looked out one after, you know, after another.
I believe at some years there were maybe 400, up to 500 children.
Graduating, maybe 25 a year or so.
They were children, most of them, as far as I know, they were children that either were born in Russia or survived in Russia.
We lived on Obinska 12, on the first floor.
Which in the United States is second, with a balcony.
And I have to say something interesting, that whenever one Jew lived in a building, he brought other friends.
And they all lived in the same building.
So over there, I had, like, a big family.
I was never home.
I was always with the neighbors.
I was the social worker for all the children.
You know, I was organizing the Purim party, and the whatever, birthday party, and babysitting for free.
There was no such thing as paying a babysitter.
You got a, you know, a nice apple or something for that for doing that.
How were the living conditions then?
Because this was right after the war.
Well, it was hard.
I did not realize how hard it was, because I was a child.
But when I look back, there were no notebooks.
There were pieces of paper or brown paper that we used.
I don't remember using a toothbrush.
I don't remember of smelling nice soap.
The dishes were washed with ashes left from the oven.
And that's how we heated our apartment.
My stepmother was unfortunately, a real stepmother.
She did not like me, because she lost her own children in Auschwitz.
And I don't know why.
In a way I do know.
She was bitter that her children did not survive.
And sometimes when I was bad, she blamed me for her children not surviving.
And she got the habit of hiding food.
So I was always hungry because she hid the food.
If she made something, she would hide part of it.
Then, you know, when it was rotten, I would find it on top of the credenza.
Yes.
I did.
I cried.
I wanted to leave.
I told my father, why did you take me out from Transtahova, where there they loved me?
They were good to me.
And my father said, because you don't belong there.
What will you be if you be in Transtahova?
You'll be a drunk, he told me.
That's the family you grew up with.
Which was true.
Everybody there was drunk.
So I stayed on.
And then I made a lot of friends in school, to which homes I used to go.
On weekends I used to sleep over.
And it took me forever to go home from school.
I took the longest route to get home late, so I won't be with my stepmother.
Did you enjoy school?
I enjoyed it very much.
It still brings tears to my eyes.
Every time I go to Poland I made a point to go to school and visit the school.
Because that was our home.
I went to that school for 10 years.
And about eight years ago, I organized a class reunion in Tel Aviv with all the children and some teachers who are still alive.
It was beautiful.
About 80.
Yeah, at the hotel of Carlton in Tel Aviv.
Yes, they did.
I found them like a little detective.
I wrote to people, and I did all kind of stuff that I can't believe today that I had the energy and the mind to do it.
Whoever I had in Poland, I wrote to them to go through the archives of the school and find out where they immigrated to.
And I brought people together from Brazil, from, from Switzerland, from Sweden, from Germany, from Poland, and mostly from Israel.
And we couldn't believe that we did not see each other for 31 years.
And three teachers did show up.
Actually, our room teacher Mr.
Brandes was there.
He wrote a beautiful poem about it, how we came together.
We left like seeds, and now we are back.
I believe from 1947 to 1957.
Breslau.
Well, when I finished the school, it's 1957. '56 already, the border opens up because of the death of Joseph Stalin in Russia.
And coming back Gomulka as a president to Poland, he opens up the border.
And we can immigrate to Israel.
Though we applied every year to immigrate to Israel, but we were always refused.
At that time, my aunt from Brazil started to write to us, my Aunt Sonia who survived in the woods.
And when we, Germany to Brazil, she wanted me to join her family to be with her.
Because I did write her unhappy letters.
And she was supposed to organize some kind of a help in Vienna, where Jews went through the transport, from Poland went through Vienna, that her friends will pick me up.
And she'll have ready passport.
And all that was arranged in a very hectic way.
And my father and my stepmother finally were planning that I will run away from the train.
And my stepmother was really happy about it.
She'd finally get rid of me.
When I came to Vienna, I did go out from the other side of the train station through a window.
And I was looking for the people who came to claim me there.
No.
They had a, a son who was married and a partner to the business of my aunt in Brazil.
They were the parents.
And they came.
And they told me very bad news, that I don't have any passport.
I have no papers.
And unfortunately, I'll have to go back to the train.
While I was away, the whole day with those people, the head of the Sochnut who had all the passports was looking for me.
And the train was stopped.
People did not-- were not able to continue their trip to Italy, where a boat would take us.
And he was very angry with my father.
He found out who was missing.
Because they count the heads.
And in the evening when I came back, the man was so angry that he almost cursed my father.
And he really gave him a curse.
He said, after this terrible times, you finally found your daughter.
And you're giving her away?
For what?
We're going to Israel, to our home.
Why you give her away to an aunt somewhere?
He said, I think that your punishment will be that you'll never have anybody to say Kaddish after you when you die.
He was very ashamed.
And we continued with the train.
And my stepmother was crying that I came back.
And all night I was standing by the window.
I didn't want to go to their compartment.
And I was looking out the window.
It was beautiful.
The Alps-- I never seen anything like it.
The roads were beautiful, clean, marked.
There was no such thing in Poland.
When I was in Vienna, I saw beautiful stores filled with goods, with apples, with fruit, with oranges, bananas.
In the windows I saw beautiful clothing.
I felt, my God, where did I live?
What is Poland?
Why do we have it so bad over there?
How long did you stay in Vienna?
Only one day.
But we continued with the train to Genoa.
And over there we were taken to a hotel, and supposed to wait for the visas to be checked.
And I believe also doctors were looking us over, who we are, and what shape we are.
And I remember the customs opening our luggage in Italy.
And they picked on one guy, that it brings tears to my eyes.
Because when he opened up that luggage, the only thing he had was one pot and a picture of his parents.
[SOBBING] That was it.
So they closed the luggage and they left everybody to go.
Because they didn't want to come across.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And of course, we came to Italy.
And there we stayed in a hotel.
It was already springtime, so the hotels were still not filled with tourists.
And there was probably around 100 people there.
We stayed there.
And the time was going by.
And we stayed another week, and another week.
In about fourth week, we were taken into buses and taken on a boat, Italian boat, going to Israel.
Everything was paid by Sochnut, the Jewish organizations that help.
And we came on a boat, very, very hard part of the voyage.
Because the sea was very, you know--
Rough.
And everybody got sick on the boat.
They couldn't eat.
Me and my father were the only ones in the restaurant coming every day to eat.
That was the first time I got to taste the olives, the pickled olives.
Which I said, how can people eat this?
This is terrible!
I spit it out.
When I came to Israel, finally, I learned to love it, that food.
But coming to Israel, finally, we arrived to the port of Haifa.
I was so excited to see beautiful buildings from far.
It didn't look anything like those postcards we got from, you know, Israel during the New Year's, where a camel was walking with a Arab or something like that, in a big Negev or whatever.
When I saw those beautiful buildings and stores right there, I was very excited.
It looked to me a little bit like Vienna.
And while my folks, my parents, were arranging things to move on and take in the luggage and everything, I was sent to meet my uncle, [NON-ENGLISH], my father's brother, and his wife.
She came.
And I was released right away.
And the aunt gave me address where to go.
She lived only two streets away.
And they went to pick up their children.
And I went by myself walking slowly toward their house.
And as I walked, everybody asked me, are you from Russia?
Are you from Russia?
I said no.
I'm from Poland.
Oh, wait a minute.
And what's your name?
So I started-- and right away, they wanted to find out if I'm their relative-- on the street.
And I asked, where do the Paluch live?
And they showed me.
Everybody spoke Yiddish or Polish on the way.
And as I came, they lived in a very [INAUDIBLE] beautiful Arabic building.
My aunt explained us.
The Arabic buildings don't need air conditioning, because they cool by themselves.
That's how they build with tiles all over.
And there were, like, three or four families living there with my aunt.
And my aunt was very sweet person.
I learned from her a lot.
She was a balabusta, first of all.
She cleaned up everything I had.
She made good food and cleaned.
And then I met my cousins when they came from school.
And that was the most exciting thing for me.
They were so sweet, so nice.
One was about age 12.
And the one little [NON-ENGLISH] that was four years old, she jumped on me.
She kissed me.
And she promised that-- I had to promise I'll sleep with her.
And in the, morning, she kissed my whole hand while I take, took her to the play school.
We fell in love right away.
I fell in love with the family.
I figured out, God, that's how it feels when you have cousins, when you have family.
It was wonderful.
My parents were sent to Beit She'an, another part of Israel, where there were a lot of Jews from Morocco.
And it was very hot in Beit She'an.
They were not used to that climate.
So my stepmother, she went to her uncle and begged him for help.
And he gave her some kind of an apartment adjoined to his factory of ice.
And they, they got to live in Tel Aviv.
But there was no room for me.
My stepmother said, look how small this is.
You cannot live here.
If she would be my real mother she would say, lay down here on the floor with me.
But she already rejected me.
And I had no place, but still with my aunt.
And at that time, that was after the Sinai War, people in Israel had to have special stamps to get food.
I didn't have yet the stamps.
So my Aunt Esther, she was giving me the food of her children, really.
Sharing.
And I felt that I am such a burden on them.
So I went every day to Sochnut and asked them about, can I go to a kibbutz?
Can I go to a place to learn Hebrew?
And everybody told me I should go to an ulpan.
And one day, they told me yes, there is space in a kibbutz Geva, and you can go there.
And I took all my possessions, whatever I had, that were not good for summer in Israel.
And I went to kibbutz Geva.
Over there I met a lot of my friends from my school, children from Egypt, children from Turkey, from Iraq, Iran.
And it was amazing to me.
I didn't know there are Jews somewhere else.
The most shocking to me was Jews from India, that they looked Indian.
They were yelling, you don't look Jewish.
I was saying, you don't look Jewish.
But we were all Jews.
Yes, I did.
I did.
Though, I remember in the beginning, when I went to school, the Jewish school, I used to stop by a church.
And slowly, slowly, I stopped.
I understood that I'm Jewish.
And I was going to a temple, which was not recommended by the Communist regime.
But since my stepmother was kind of a religious person, she made sure that I went to the shul on Rosh Hashanah, on Yom Kippur, and some Fridays.
I did learn Yiddish.
But everybody was making fun of me, because my Yiddish had a Polish accent.
So they were saying that, you better don't say anything.
Speak Polish, and we'll talk to you Yiddish.
But I didn't like it.
I did learn Yiddish.
I even participated in some plays in school of Yiddish.
And I did realize I am Jewish.
And that's who I am.
And I-- at that age, a teenager, I learned a little bit from history, but not much.
My education came later.
Oh, I have to tell you before that, what happened.
I did-- we were supposed to-- that was the rule, that we were supposed to study Hebrew half a day and then work a little bit and help out in the kibbutz, because we did not pay a penny.
And I loved that life in the kibbutz.
First day, they put me to press shirts.
I didn't know even where to begin.
But somebody sat there and showed me how to do it correctly.
The next day, they took me to the kitchen.
And there I learned how to wash dishes, cook [INAUDIBLE], how to do things that I didn't know from my home.
Because we didn't have all those, you know, detergents that they have.
We were quite behind.
And then one day I was supposed to play with children, be there at night babysitting.
And it was the nicest experience.
Because the children were laying down.
And they saying, will you tell us about yourself?
Ida, oh, they gave me a name, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], will you tell us how was in Poland?
Will you be my friend?
You know, those little voices.
I loved that life in, in kibbutz.
But unfortunately, it wasn't so.
You know, there was a, a different way to come out from there.
